seoTitle: How to Create a Spike in Jira
metaDescription: Learn how to set up a spike issue in Jira to track research or investigation work that needs to happen before a team can estimate a story.
intro: A spike is a time-boxed research task used when a team needs to investigate something before they can properly estimate or plan a piece of work. This guide covers how to create and track a spike as an issue in Jira.

steps.heading: Decide if a spike is the right issue type
steps.body: Spikes are typically used when there's too much uncertainty to estimate a story accurately, and the team needs dedicated time to research or prototype first.

steps.heading: Create a new issue
steps.body: Start a new issue in your Jira project, since most teams don't have a built-in 'Spike' issue type by default.

steps.heading: Label or type it as a spike
steps.body: Use a label, custom issue type, or naming convention to mark the issue clearly as a spike so it's distinguishable from regular stories.

steps.heading: Define the time box
steps.body: Set a clear time limit for the investigation in the issue description, since a spike is meant to be bounded rather than open-ended.

steps.heading: Document findings when complete
steps.body: Once the research is done, record the outcome on the issue so the team can use it to estimate or plan the follow-up work.

faq.question: Does Jira have a built-in spike issue type?
faq.answer: Not by default in most configurations, so teams commonly create a custom issue type or use a label to track spikes.

faq.question: How is a spike different from a regular story?
faq.answer: A spike is focused on research or investigation rather than delivering a feature, and it's meant to inform estimation on future work.

faq.question: Should a spike be included in sprint velocity?
faq.answer: Practices vary by team, but many teams choose not to count spike effort toward velocity since it's investigative rather than delivery work.

faq.question: What happens after a spike is finished?
faq.answer: The findings typically feed into creating or refining a related story, which can then be estimated more accurately.
